title: What is a variable
instruction:
A variable is a labeled box. You put a value in it, stick a label on it, and use that label whenever you need the value.

In Python, creating a variable is one line:

`name = "Alex"`

The `=` sign means *store this value in this variable*. It is not asking if they are equal — it is an instruction.

Run this.
successMessage: name contains "Alex". print(name) printed the value inside the box — not the word 'name', but what name holds.

title: Create your own variable
instruction:
Create a variable called `name` and store your own name in it. Then print it.

Remember: text values need quotes. `name = Alex` will crash. `name = "Alex"` works.
starter: # TODO: create a variable called name with your name, then print it

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Your own variable with your own name. Every program you ever write will be full of variables exactly like this.

title: Variables hold numbers too
instruction:
Variables can hold any type of value — not just text.

Numbers do not need quotes: `age = 25` (not `age = "25"`).

Create a variable `age` with your age and a variable `year` with the current year. Print both.
starter: # TODO: create age and year variables, then print them

codeChecks.message: Create a variable: age = 25 (use your actual age)
Create a variable: year = 2025 (or current year)
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Numbers need no quotes. age = 25 stores the number 25. age = "25" would store text — and you cannot do math with text.

title: Reassigning a variable
instruction:
Variables are not fixed. You can change the value inside at any time.

Run this. Notice that `score` prints as 60 first, then 95 after reassignment. The old value is simply replaced.
successMessage: 60 then 95. The variable updated. This is why they are called variables — the value varies. You will reassign variables constantly in real programs.

title: Update using the current value
instruction:
You can use a variable's current value to compute its next value:

`count = count + 1` — add 1 to whatever count currently holds.

Run this. Then add two more lines that increase `score` by 10 each time and print it.
hint:
score = score + 10
print(score)
score = score + 10
print(score)
starter:
score = 50
print(score)
# TODO: add 10 to score twice, printing after each addition

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 50 → 60 → 70. The variable holds the running value each time. This pattern — update a variable using itself — is everywhere in programming.

title: Multiple variables on one line
instruction:
Python lets you assign multiple variables at once:

`first, last = "Alex", "Rivera"`

This is called tuple unpacking. The values on the right are matched in order to the names on the left.

Create `city` and `country` in one line, then print both.
starter: # TODO: assign city and country in one line, then print them

codeChecks.message: Assign both on one line: city, country = "City", "Country"
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Two variables, one line. Python unpacks from left to right — city gets the first value, country gets the second.

title: Swap two variables
instruction:
In most languages, swapping two variables requires a temporary third variable:

```
temp = a
a = b
b = temp
```

Python can do it in one line:

`a, b = b, a`

Run this. Then swap `first` and `last` the Python way.
starter:
first = "Alex"
last = "Rivera"
print(first, last)
# TODO: swap first and last in one line
print(first, last)

codeChecks.message: Swap them: first, last = last, first
Replace the TODO comment with your swap.

successMessage: Alex Rivera → Rivera Alex. One line swap. Python evaluates the right side completely before assigning — no temporary variable needed.

title: Naming rules
instruction:
Variable names have rules:
- Only letters, numbers, and underscores (`_`)
- Cannot start with a number
- Case-sensitive: `score` and `Score` are different
- Convention: use `snake_case` (words separated by underscores)

The code below has three broken variable names. Fix all three.
hint: 1stPlace → first_place, full name → full_name, user-score → user_score
starter:
# BUG: these variable names are invalid — fix them
1stPlace = "Gold"
full name = "Alex Rivera"
user-score = 99

print(first_place)
print(full_name)
print(user_score)
successMessage: Three valid names. snake_case is Python's convention — lowercase words joined by underscores. You will write thousands of variable names in your career.

title: del — remove a variable
instruction:
`del` removes a variable. After deletion, trying to use it causes a `NameError`.

Run this. Then add a line that deletes `temp` and prints a message confirming it's gone.
starter:
temp = "I am temporary"
print(temp)
# TODO: delete temp and print "temp deleted"

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: del removes the variable entirely from memory. Not commonly needed day-to-day, but useful for cleaning up large objects or making code intent clear.

title: Build a profile
instruction:
No starter code.

Create four variables:
- `name` — your name
- `age` — your age
- `city` — your city
- `language` — `"Python"`

Then print all four, each on its own line.

codeChecks.message: Create a name variable with your name.
Create an age variable with your age.

successMessage: Four variables, four print calls, a complete mini-profile. Variables are the foundation of every program. Chapter 2 done.
